# Lenny Rachitsky’s Post
Lenny Rachitsky
2y
Great interview with Brian Chesky, including Brian clarifying what's happening with product management at Airbnb. Here's the relevant clip (including a very kind shout-out), but I encourage you to listen to the whole thing: https://lnkd.in/ewGUM-bk"My comments were tweeted a little out of context. I said we got rid of the classic product management function. And so people assumed I got rid of all the product managers. I didn't. What I meant was we replaced the role traditional PM role with product marketing. Product marketing at Airbnb is inbound and outbound. *The role is now product management plus product marketing*."
Alex Kantrowitz
2y
Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky got heated as we talked cleaning fees in his company’s New York City offices yesterday. We were debating whether Airbnb should display total price per night by default, and Chesky jumped into a breakdown of hospitality industry products and practices before precisely explaining how Airbnb fit and its tradeoffs. “I actually stay in the details of the product,” he told me. “CEOs at S&P 500, Fortune 100 companies, I think most of them aren't really in the details of their product. At some point, these companies get so big, that it's pretty typical that you get a little detached. That it's someone else's job to listen to customers. But it's not your job. And I've always wanted to be the kind of company that felt more like a startup.” After admitting he was once a bit too removed from customers, Chesky is clearly all in on product now. During the conversation, now live on Big Technology Podcast, he announced the results of Twitter (or X) survey he posted, asking customers for feedback on the product. Their suggestions about cleaning fees, listing reliability, and customer services led to real product results. Some big changes included more than 260,000 Airbnb properties lowering or removing cleaning fees, verified listings spinning up in five countries, and pet friendly listings — and even king beds — getting added to search. Autonomy and removed leadership is kind of in vogue in the tech world right now, but Chesky is putting himself in the middle of the process. Consider: 👉 He’s turned product management into product marketing as he and his executive team have gotten more involved in building. 👉 Airbnb runs an Apple-style development process, putting hardware lessons into play at a software company, and shipping big updates twice per year. 👉 Chesky says the company’s shipped 250-300 new updates since moving to a new model where he’s been more involved. “The velocity is somewhere between 2 and 5x greater than it was before,” he said. “The quality of the product is better.” Do you think Chesky’s approach is working? Is it good for CEOs to be this engaged? From my vantage point, Chesky's interaction with customers is really notable, and will pay off in the long run. And in the short term, I’d still recommend a cap on cleaning fees, or perhaps an outright ban. Give the podcast a listen, share with a friend, and let me hear your feedback the comments! I’m all in the details now too ;) Apple: https://lnkd.in/eHSBFwiP Spotify: https://lnkd.in/e-mFpcZr

# How to be a successful CEO at Airbnb
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Pete Sena
1y
From Broke founder to tech billionaire. Brian Chesky has a net worth of over $8.4 billion. We can all learn and apply these lessons for our own growth. Together with his team, they built: • A ubiquitous global brand we all know and love • An industry that disrupted hospitality forever • A strong company culture • A customer obsessed brand • A scalable platform serving billions of people We learn alot when we listen deeply. Lessons in his words on how you can do it too: 1. Focus on One Person at a time ↳"If you want to create a great product, just focus on one person. Make that one person have the most amazing experience ever." 2. Survive the Storm ↳"Customers are willing to try new things, and if you can survive, you will have fewer competitors. It's like entering the eye of the storm. As long as you are strong enough to survive, you can end up in still water by yourself." 3. Strong Culture Matters ↳"There is no such thing as a good or bad culture. It's either a strong or weak culture." 4. Personal Touch in Hiring ↳"I interviewed the first 300 employees at Airbnb." 5. Slow Start, Big Finish ↳"Airbnb launched and a year after we launched, I think we had 100 people/day visiting our website and we had like 2 bookings." 6. Your main job as a CEO ↳"Somebody asked me 'what's the job of a CEO,' and there's a number of things a CEO does. What you mostly do is articulate the vision, develop the strategy, and you gotta hire people to fit the culture. If you do those three things, you basically have a company. And that company will hopefully be successful, if you have the right vision, the right strategy, and good people." 7. Think Long-Term ↳"Think about it this way- if your company is like your child — a parent wants his child to outlive him or her." 8. Build for Love ↳"Build something 100 people love, not something 1 million people kind of like." 9. Start Small ↳"Airbnb was never meant to be the big idea. It was meant to be the thing that paid the rent so we could think of the big idea." 10. Join the Conversation ↳"Whatever the press is talking about, they want to keep talking about it. So instead of asking yourself, 'How can I get them to start talking about me?,' figure out a way to get yourself involved in what they're already talking about."

How Airbnb's CEO Earns $1 Salary | Bernardo Brega posted on the topic | LinkedIn

# How Airbnb's CEO Earns $1 Salary
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Bernardo Brega
5mo
“Show Me the Incentives”. Airbnb’s Masterclass in Alignment. Quoting Charlie Munger: “Show me the incentives, and I’ll show you the outcome.” What does that has to do with Airbnb? We’ve been researching Airbnb lately, and Brian Chesky, the founder and CEO, has one of the most interesting compensation plans I’ve ever seen. He literally earns a $1 salary. His entire compensation is tied to a 10-year stock-based plan, defined at the time of Airbnb’s IPO in late 2020. --- How it works: The plan includes 10 tranches of 1.2 million Airbnb shares each, vesting between 2021 and 2030. • Each tranche only vests if Airbnb’s stock price sustains specific milestones. (image below) • The first two tranches have already vested. • The rest depend on the company compounding meaningfully over the decade. • For him to get all of them the stock must compound at 32% / year for the next 5 years. • Once a tranche vests, there’s a two-year delay before he can receive the shares. In simple terms: if the stock price doesn’t grow, if the company doesn’t actually create long-term shareholder value, Brian Chesky gets nothing. No quarterly bonuses. No salary bumps. No “retention” awards to keep him comfortable. Just a single, long bet on Airbnb’s future. (*disclosure, he already owns 13.5% of the company) --- Why It Matters: Most CEO pay structures pretend to be performance-based but are really time-based. Or, they create bad incentives such as inflating revenues disregarding profitability. Stay long enough, survive enough quarters, and you’ll get paid… The outcome has little importance (crazy) Chesky’s plan is different. It is about compounding. He only wins if everyone else wins. And he only wins big if Airbnb becomes one of the world’s truly great businesses. (the market cap of the company if he hits the goal in 2030 would be something like 300bn usd vs 75bn today). That’s what alignment looks like in practice. --- The man behind the plan: What makes Chesky interesting is that this incentive structure matches who he is as a leader. He’s not a “visionary CEO” in the abstract sense. He’s hands-on. Detail-oriented to a fault. He still reviews design mockups himself. He obsesses over the user experience. He’s also Airbnb’s Head of Product and Design. He doesn’t do one-on-one meetings with his direct reports. He says, “If I do that, it becomes therapy for the person, and I’m not a therapist.” He spends much of his time on hiring, which he calls the single most important job of a CEO. He once said that early on, he’d personally interview the first 1,000 employees at Airbnb, because getting culture right is non-negotiable. All of that points to one thing: long-term stewardship.

"$2M ARR in three months used to be impressive. Now we expect it within ten days. Right now, momentum is the only moat." This tweet by a16z's investor Bryan Kim went super viral this week, with a lot of founders & vcs losing their mind saying "Momentum is not a moat" This is my take: At the beginning you don’t have defensibility. There is nothing to defend. You earn the right to have a moat later. Momentum → adoption → scale → moat And there are only four real defensibilities in tech: 1. Network effects The product becomes more valuable as more people use it. Example: Airbnb. Hosts attract guests, guests attract more hosts. You don’t get this at the start — you get it after enough usage concentrates in one place. 2. Brand People default to you without comparing. Example: when you think of booking a hotel, Booking is top of mind. That wasn’t there on day one. It was built by repeated distribution and familiarity. 3. Embedding The product gets wired into a workflow, so ripping it out is painful. Example: Stripe. Once it sits inside your checkout and billing logic, it is easier to scale with Stripe than replace it. 4. Economies of scale The bigger you get, the more leverage you gain. Example: Amazon. Volume gives you cheaper logistics, more selection, better delivery, which attracts more users, which increases volume again. None of these show up at $0 to $1M ARR. They only form after scale. Imagine asking Airbnb for its moat in the first year. There was none. What makes it defensible today is its user base of landlords and its brand, both created through explosive growth What do you think?

Reid Hoffman invested in Airbnb when every other VC said no. His partner David Sze told him: "Every VC has to have a deal they're going to fail on. Airbnb can be yours." The risks were obvious: 1. Staying in strangers' houses felt weird 2. Cities would hate it 3. Hotel lobbies would try to outlaw it 4. Transaction volume was microscopic But Reid saw something different. He understood a principle most investors miss: ↳ Smart people thinking your idea is bad isn't a bug. It's a feature. Here's why: When brilliant people see your vision as flawed, you get: → Years of no competition → Time to build your network effects → Space to establish the marketplace → Lower entry costs Before anyone realizes it's working. The contrarian bet isn't about being different for the sake of it. It's about having a theory for why smart skeptics are wrong. Reid had plans for each risk. He knew most could make Airbnb worth zero. But if it worked? Industry transformation. 6 months later, Airbnb's hockey stick appeared. David came back and asked: "What did you see that I didn't?" Reid's answer: "You were right about all the risks. But we do a portfolio because some bets can be huge if they work." The lesson? If everyone thinks your idea is great, you probably don't have a big idea. If smart people think it's terrible, you might be onto something. The question isn't: "Will this succeed?" The question is: "Do I have a theory for why the smart skeptics are wrong?" That's where the biggest opportunities hide.

In 2007, Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia were struggling to pay rent in San Francisco. They noticed there was a big conference coming to town, and hotels were fully booked. They thought, what if we rent out air mattresses in our living room and provide breakfast? They launched AirBed & Breakfast, a humble, scrappy idea. They pitched the concept to investors. Rejected. Get feedback early. Pivot doesn’t mean abandon your core. Airbnb shifted design, trust, UX not its core mission. Persistence and iteration beats brilliance. They were far from perfect at the start. If you’re building something now, a service, product, pitch, don’t wait for it to be bulletproof. Release, test, learn. What small version of your vision could you release today, just to test whether people care?

### From three airbeds in San Francisco to an AI-powered experience platform, Airbnb’s journey is more than a business story — it’s a masterclass in founder-driven resilience, vision, and reinvention.
---
### 🛫 “We Are Unkillable”: Airbnb’s Pandemic Pivot
Let’s rewind to March 2020.
80% of Airbnb’s business disappears almost overnight. Global lockdowns begin. Travel halts. Most people counted Airbnb out.
But Brian Chesky didn't. Instead of scrambling or posturing, he made a decision: Airbnb would not die on his watch. He told his board, his team, and himself: "We are unkillable."
And then he went to work.
---
### 📉 From a $35B Booking Business to Freefall — and Back
While most startups would have entered panic mode, Chesky entered rebuild mode.
- He refunded $1B to guests, even overriding host cancellation policies.
- He sent $250M to hosts—out of Airbnb’s own bank account.
- He personally reviewed every one of the 7,000+ employees laid off, and wrote what is now considered one of the most human layoff letters ever.
- He raised $2B in emergency debt, rather than accept a dilutive equity round.
- He shut down 80% of Airbnb’s products, including flights, hotels, and magazines.
The man didn’t blink. He went from scaling an empire to restarting from scratch, without losing the company's soul. That’s founder DNA.
---
### 💡 The Reset: Product-Led, Principle-Driven
Airbnb emerged from the pandemic leaner, sharper, and… better.
- Chesky flattened the org and reverted to a functional structure.
- He demanded expertise—engineering managers had to code again.
- He instilled "shared consciousness" from the top 30 to 300 to 3,000 people.
- He reviewed every product before launch.
- And yes—he even wrote 14,000 words of the S1 filing.
What I admire most?
He brought the company back to its founding purpose: building a world where anyone can belong anywhere.
---
### 🚀 Q2 2025: Proof That the Vision Is Working
Let’s talk numbers. Airbnb is no longer just back—it's thriving:
- $3.1B in revenue (+13% YoY)
- $1B in free cash flow
- 60,000+ new applications for hosting services and experiences
- AI-powered customer support has already cut human contact volume by 15%
But this isn’t just financial success.
Airbnb is now more than a booking platform—it’s becoming a travel OS:
- Experiences curated by locals (ride a sidecar in Paris, cook with a grandmother in Tuscany)
- On-demand services (massage therapists, chefs, hair stylists delivered to your Airbnb)
- A redesigned app with personalized recommendations before, during, and after a trip
This is not a pivot. It’s a second act.
---
### 🤖 The AI Move Everyone Else Overlooked
Most travel companies are leaning on AI to inspire you. Airbnb is using it to serve you.
Rather than dream up your next vacation, their AI agent helps manage, resolve, and even act on your needs in real time. It’s trained on 13 models and tens of thousands of conversations.
As Chesky put it:
> “It won’t just tell you how to cancel a reservation. It’ll know which one, cancel it for you, and help you rebook.”
It's not just a chatbot. It’s a true AI concierge. And it’s working.
---
### 🏰 Airbnb ⟶ Disneybnb?
Yes, Chesky said it himself. He wants Airbnb to be used once a week, not once a year. Sound crazy?
Amazon started with books. Now you buy toothpaste and groceries. Airbnb started with airbeds. Now it’s a lifestyle platform.

The idea is so compelling that Fast Company suggested Chesky could be Bob Iger’s successor at Disney. While I doubt he’d ever leave his baby, the comparison isn't wrong.
Both Airbnb and Disney are in the experience economy. Only now, Airbnb is delivering real memories, in real life, curated by locals, powered by AI, and guided by trust.
And while Disney partners with the NFL and ESPN to rethink media, maybe Airbnb is doing the same for travel, work, and living.
---
### 🔁 The Timeline That Should Be Studied in Every Founder Playbook
If you haven’t seen this already, watch this interview. It’s required viewing for anyone building something real.
### Detailed Timeline of Airbnb's Journey
2007:
- Fall: Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia host three people at their San Francisco apartment during a design conference. This "magical, unique experience" plants the seed for their idea.
- Late 2007 - Early 2008: The initial idea, "Airbed & Breakfast," is conceived. The co-founders struggle for a year to get the marketplace going, facing difficulties finding guests and raising money (receiving "tons of NOs"). They experience inconsistent "Y-axis of growth."
2008:
- March: Joe and Brian attend South by Southwest. Initially, only airbeds can be rented due to the company name. A user expresses interest in renting an "extra bedroom" without an airbed, leading to the first expansion beyond airbeds to include real mattresses, though breakfast remains a requirement to uphold the "Airbed & Breakfast" name.
- Fall: As a "last ditch effort," Brian Chesky decides to apply to Y Combinator (YC). Nate Blecharczyk, the technical co-founder, agrees to participate in YC and move from Boston to the Bay Area for three months, with the understanding that if it doesn't work, they're "officially done."
- During YC: The co-founders commit to a rigorous schedule: waking at 8 AM, working, exercising together, grocery shopping, and working until midnight. Sundays are dedicated to weekly recaps and planning.
- They embrace Paul Graham's "Ramen profitable" concept, visualizing this threshold on their bathroom mirror as a red line, representing the minimum subsistence level to keep the company afloat without external funding.
- Paul Graham challenges them to go to New York City to meet their users, despite their concern about scalability. Brian and Joe go door-to-door, personally meeting hosts.
- Brian carries a bank ledger and pens in his backpack to manually write checks to hosts, demonstrating the "unscalable" initial operations.
- They notice the poor quality of listing photos (due to bad 2009 camera phones). This leads to the concept of "magical service" through on-demand photographers. Brian or Joe would show up with rented cameras, eventually hiring contractors, creating one of the earliest on-demand platforms (predating Uber by a year).
- They learn to create large photos for product description pages, a novelty compared to Craigslist's small 2x2 photo tiles.
- Airbnb becomes one of the first sites to integrate online booking and payment between users (unlike Etsy or eBay, which used separate PayPal transactions).
- Nate hacks together a tool to repost Airbnb listings onto Craigslist, allowing users to click through to Airbnb from Craigslist.
- Greg McAdoo from Sequoia Capital attends a YC dinner. He informs the Airbnb founders that the "vacation rental industry is a $40 billion industry," an insight that shifts their understanding of their market size beyond "airbeds." Sequoia funds them.
- A key internal debate arises regarding allowing hosts to rent out their entire homes, challenging the "breakfast" aspect of "Airbed & Breakfast." The decision is made to drop the requirement for the host to be present and make breakfast, leading to the platform evolving to allow full apartment/home rentals.
- Airbnb's motto expands from "couch to a castle" to include unique listings such as treehouses, castles, private islands, and igloos, continually pushing the boundaries of what can be rented.
- They exit YC feeling like "winners," having achieved momentum and secured funding from Sequoia.
2009:
- First week of February: Revenue hits $468.
- Second week of February: Revenue reaches $897.
- Third week of February: Revenue reaches $1,428, making them "Ramen profitable."
Throughout the 2010s (pre-pandemic):
- Airbnb grows into a "rocket ship," one of the "hottest companies."
- They expand, adding divisions for magazines, transportation, flights, business travel, pro hosts, experiences, luxury, and social impact (air.org).
- The company adopts a decentralized, functional structure that empowers teams and democratizes data.
- They raise "too much money" and hire "too many people," leading to a slower pace and less focus.
- They operate at near break-even, losing approximately $250 million a year.
Late 2019 - Early 2020:
- Brian Chesky spends the holidays working on Airbnb's S1 document, preparing to go public.
2020 (Pandemic Crisis):
- Late January: Airbnb's business in China experiences a precipitous drop (80%) due to COVID-19.
- Mid-February: COVID-19 is still largely perceived as a China-specific issue.
- March 15th (or around that week): The world begins to shut down, symbolized by the NBA suspending its season.
- March (Emergency Board Meeting): Ken Chenault (lead independent board member and former CEO of American Express) calls Brian, stating his intuition that this crisis will be "10 times the size of 9/11 or 2008." Brian commits to the mantra "we are unkillable."
- Within 8 weeks: Airbnb loses 80% of its $35 billion annual booking volume.
- Crisis Response (following YC principles): Leadership & Psychology: Brian focuses on maintaining his own optimistic and resilient mindset to lead the company.
- Communication: Daily stand-ups with the executive team; weekly All-Hands Q&As (despite advice not to); weekly board meetings; daily calls with executives and weekly calls with board members. Brian ensures total transparency and brings employees "on the journey."
- Focus & Principles: Realizing they were "deprived" of their core mission, Brian clarifies the company's purpose: building community in the physical world. He ruthlessly cuts "fake work" and distractions, adhering to core principles instead of data (which is absent in a crisis).
- Restructuring & Efficiency: They shutter 80% of their products and divisions (e.g., magazine, transportation, flights, business travel).
- Half of the executive team turns over.
- Salaries of leadership are removed.
- Customer Refunds: They refund $1 billion of customer deposits, overriding host cancellation policies.
- Host Support: They take $250 million from their bank and send it to hosts.
- Layoffs: Brian personally reviews every single one of the approximately 7,000 employees laid off, making the decisions himself with compassion. He writes an "uncharacteristically open" letter explaining the situation.
- Funding: They raised $2 billion in emergency debt financing (from Silver Lake and Sixth Street Partners) as a high-stakes gamble, avoiding a highly dilutive equity round.
- Operational Refounding :They revert from a divisionalized structure to a functional one (marketing, operations, engineering, design).
- Brian personally reviews "every single thing" before it ships (Co-reviews).
- A "shared consciousness" is fostered among the top 30, then 300, then 3,000 people, focusing on a single roadmap.
- Brian demands expertise from managers (e.g., engineering managers must code).
- He personally writes 14,000 words of the S1.
- The company is run like a "hardware startup" due to its discipline and focus.
- Post-Crisis (Emergence): Airbnb emerges stronger, financially sound, and highly efficient.
- Employee morale remains high; almost no one significant resigns during the crisis.
- The company generates $3.8 billion in free cash flow (last year mentioned), achieving a "unicorn by profit" status four times over, with a 44% free cash flow margin (higher than Google or Apple).
- Headcount is kept flat for years, fostering an environment akin to "Navy Seals" or "Special Forces" – a lean, fast-moving "world's biggest startup."
Post-Pandemic (Public Company):
- Airbnb goes public. Brian finds running a public company CEO "less dramatic" than running a private company during the pandemic.
- The company implements semi-annual "giant product releases" (like a hardware company) to focus the entire company's efforts, diverting attention from daily stock price fluctuations.
- Brian reports having "more energy" and enjoying his job more than during YC, leveraging an "incredible canvas" with his team.
---
### So What Can Founders Learn From Brian Chesky?
1. It’s okay to start unscalable. They knocked on doors, wrote physical checks, and took photos themselves. That’s how you learn.
2. Crisis is clarity. Layoffs, refunds, and restructures aren’t just financial decisions—they’re character decisions.
3. Know when to kill your darlings. Chesky shut down 80% of the company in a few weeks. That’s what focus looks like.
4. Repetition is reputation. He didn’t reinvent the wheel—he doubled down on trust, hospitality, and storytelling.
5. Founders need to stay close to the product. He’s still reviewing designs. Still writing docs. Still hands-on.
---
### Final Thought
I’ve admired Chesky since the day he found out, on camera, what Airbnb was worth before its IPO opened. The humility, the awe, the disbelief. He built a global brand, but never stopped thinking like a founder.
That’s what sticks with me.
Not the scale.
The soul.
---
✍️ If you’re a founder, operator, or builder—bookmark this. Rewatch the interview. Study the timeline. And maybe ask yourself: How would I lead if 80% of my business were to disappear overnight?
The best leaders don’t just survive. They reimagine what’s possible.

Nailed it! Airbnb co-founder & CEO Brian Chesky brilliantly explains the three stages of building a successful company: “When you start a company you go through three stages. The first stage is when you build the product and that stage ends when you get to product market fit. Then stage two starts. And stage two is now you build the company that grows the product. So you get to hire people and this is typically when you're raising a lot of money. And stage three is you become kind of more professional, a little more administrative and you hire an executive team. If you succeed in doing all of that, you go back to step one which is to create a new product” - Brian Chesky For the best companies, this cycle never ends. Because as Steve Jobs once wrote to Apple employees, you're only as good as your next amazing new product. After helping dozens of founders validate, they all ask: When is the right time to launch? Here’s what I tell them. Short answer: ASAP. Long answer: Don’t overthink it. Early launches will reveal the truth. Even an unpolished version of your product will tell you if you’re solving a real problem. One that people care to pay for. AND, the worst case scenario? Isn’t bad at all! Maybe people think your product sucks. Good. That’s your opportunity to learn. Ask deeper questions. Find out what the real pain is. Two things can happen: 1. Great execution isn't accidental; it's my team constantly adjusting, fixing, and managing chaos in real time. This experience changed how I lead. Rapid Recap: Brian Chesky on Airbnb’s Next Evolution & the Future of Travel
Agree & Join LinkedIn
Airbnb is on the verge of a major transformation, says co-founder and CEO Brian Chesky. In a conversation with Rapid Response host Bob Safian, Chesky shares how the company is preparing to evolve beyond short-term stays, laying the foundation for a broader vision of travel and living.
“This is the moment where we want to expand the Airbnb model beyond a place to stay,” Chesky shares. “Often in Silicon Valley, ideas are too early, and we probably weren’t ready to expand beyond our core business until this moment.”
To make this leap, Chesky says the company has undertaken a massive effort — rebuilding its app from the ground up, restructuring internally, and developing new functions — to support the expansion to longer-term stays and into new categories of travel and living.
Chesky describes it as a challenge that keeps the company dynamic: “It keeps us from becoming a big, old company by having to fundamentally change and reinvent who we are.”
He likened Airbnb’s trajectory to Amazon’s evolution from an online bookseller to a retail giant.
> “There are literally hundreds of different verticals that one could go into. We probably won’t go into all of them. It’s really about abstracting the Airbnb platform to be able to go into more things than just housing.”
One key priority is elevating quality, Chesky says. Airbnb will shift from an open marketplace to one known for trust and excellence, introducing verification systems and a quality seal of approval. “When you see this, you’ll know we vetted this, we stand behind this, and we have a guarantee of quality. I think Airbnb going from an open marketplace to a marketplace that’s known for excellence and quality, that’s another vector.”
This shift, he believes, “would be the most revolutionary thing we did.”

“This is by some measures, the loneliest time in human history.” Brian Chesky joined Rapid Response host Bob Safian on the Masters of Scale Summit stage to discuss the loneliness epidemic driven by the overuse of screens and social media — and how Airbnb is helping people reconnect in real life. Transcript Transcript
##### Transcript
This is, by some measures, the loneliest time in human history. I mean, loneliness is rising throughout the world. In 2012, social networking became social media. Your friends became your followers. And then performance replace intimacy. We're staring at a piece of glass all day. We can find ourselves feeling more connected than ever, but actually feeling more isolated than ever before. It feels like every innovation is a step forward for humanity. And yet somehow there's a larger cost. There's a larger price that we're paying. And the price we're paying is, I just think we're like, a little more. Slater We're more divided, we're a little more lonely. There's not a sense of community, there's not a sense of belonging. Real connection happens in the real world. People want to feel like they're a part of something. They belong. It's a why are we doing all this? Like, what are we building this towards? I'd like to be part of the solution, not part of the problem. I want Airbnb to be the company gets you off your phone, not on your phone. We want to make the world feel smaller. I want to build not an app, but a community. You'll use the app to get the community. We're going to invest in AI, but it's not about that. It's a means to building. Community, because the only thing you can belong to is a community or a tribe or a family. We want to build a global community where you can travel anywhere, live anywhere and belong anywhere because I think that's what the world needs.
Loneliness & isolation are how some clients feel before I help coach them through spending less time on their phone. Once they go from 6 hours --> 3 hours per day, they feel more present, have better attention and actually spent more time on 'meaningful' connection. If you want to solve your own loneliness, consider changing your relationship with your phone.

Brian Chesky explains how Airbnb solved the chicken-and-egg problem “Marketplaces are incredibly defensible at scale, and maybe it’s because they’re incredibly hard to start. And the problem is simple - they call it the chicken and egg problem.” As Brian explains, it was tough to bootstrap Airbnb in the beginning because travelers couldn’t book homes if there was no inventory, and homeowners didn’t want to list their homes unless people were going to book them. “We didn’t know what to do for a while .We tried a lot of different things. And I can tell you what worked. Summer of 2008, the press announces that Barack Obama is moving from a 20,000 seat basketball arena to an 80,000 seat football stadium. And we said, that’s our shot. You have 60,000 people that don’t have housing, surely at least a few of them are going to need a place to stay… And so we literally started with local people in Denver. Then we started emailing bloggers. We got the bloggers. Then the Denver Post and the Rocky Mountain News covered us. Then the local ABC and NBC and CBS affiliates. And then the Wall Street Journal. Then the New York Times and CNN are in our living room… We did that in a matter of three weeks.” Brian continues: “We started these little infernos. You start getting a few users here, a hundred here, fifty there… And we did the same thing with the inauguration. And when you have a hundred people here and there, then you obsessively meet them… Paul Graham, our first investor, said it’s better to have a hundred people love you than a million people kind of like you. And the reason why is it’s really hard to build off of a really wide but shallow base. But with a hundred people, you can find out everything they want… You meet them, you spend a ton of time with them, and once they fall in love with your product, they’ll tell every one of their friends. That’s why [Airbnb] took a really long time to start, but it grew much faster later on.” Video source: Commonwealth Club World Affairs(2011)

And we said, ohh, that's our shot. You have 60,000 people don't have housing, Surely at least a few of them are gonna need a place to stay. And it was really in the spirit of the Obama campaign that people wanted to stay with other people, kind of a grassroots movement. And so we went literally, we started with like local people in Denver, then we started emailing bloggers. We got the bloggers, then the Denver, like Denver Post and the Rocky Mountain News covered us, then the local ABC and NBC and CBS affiliates, and then Wall Street Journal, then New York Times. And eventually CNN is like in our living room over the course of three weeks. And we did that and it literally went from a matter of three weeks, went from a few guys in apartment, no business, no money, completely broke with no press to a few guys in the living room with still no business, no money can still completely broke, maybe even more broke. But now we're on CNN, New York Times and that kind of started these little like infernos. So you start getting a few users here, 100 here, 50 there, 100 there with the same thing with inauguration. And we have 100 people here and there. Then you obsessively meet them. And the great thing this last thing I'll say is this. One of our other advisors named Paul Graham, he's our first investor, he said. It's better to have 100 people love you than a million people kind of like you. And the reason why is. It's really hard to like have this like really wide, shallow base and to continue to build off him. You'll never even meet them. But Andrew people, you can find out everything they want. Most of these people never even met somebody started a website. You meet them, you spend a ton of time with them and once they fall in love with you literally as a product, they'll tell everyone of their friends. And that's why it took a really long time to start, but it grew much faster later on.

Ever stayed in an Airbnb and caught yourself thinking, “This is mine for now… I’ll just move a few things around to make it cozier”? Turns out, those small acts might say a lot about how we think about ownership in an access-based rental context even when we don’t actually own the thing. In our paper, "Psychological Ownership and Territorial Behaviors in Rental Transactions: Why “Who” You Rent from Matters" (https://lnkd.in/eM9TNNx3), Sarah Whitley and I found something fascinating across nine experiments (and many hours 😅): It is not just what we rent that matters, but who we rent it from. When people rent from a company, they actually feel more ownership over the product, almost like, “this is mine for now.” And that feeling drives what we call territorial behaviors such as moving furniture, hosting gatherings, or even leaving negative reviews when access is restricted in rental properties, highlighting sections in a rental book or off-routing in a rental car. But when the exact same product is rented from a person, those behaviors are less likely to be engaged in. People seem to hold back perhaps because they sense the personal connection the owner has with that space. And for managers, that means something powerful: how a provider presents themselves—as a person or a company—changes how consumers perceive their connection to the original product. Why was this project so much fun to work on? 1️⃣ We used a mixed-methods approach. We scraped hundreds of Airbnb provider comments and found that territorial behaviors—like moving furniture or hosting parties—cost hosts real time and money. 2️⃣ We spoke directly with Airbnb providers and learned that time and effort spent cleaning or resetting spaces were major pain points. Suddenly, territorial behavior wasn’t just an abstract theoretical concept, it was very real. 3️⃣ We even ran a lab study with real books (!!!!) We printed actual poetry books, labeled them as owned by either a company or a person, and observed whether participants marked or highlighted in them. They did and exactly as predicted! Why should you read it? Because it connects three fascinating streams of work: (1) Access-based consumption (2) Psychological ownership (3) Territorial behaviors And I cannot end this post with thanking my amazing co-author (Sarah Whitley). It was the most fun experience and also full of deep learning moments! Fleura Bardhi, Giana Eckhardt, Suzanne Shu, Joann Peck, Colleen P. Kirk, Carey Morewedge John Costello Rebecca Walker Reczek Aaron Brough Remi Trudel Vanitha Swaminathan(might find this relevant and interesting). D'Amore-McKim School of Business at Northeastern University Questrom School of Business, Boston University Thanks to Journal of Marketing for covering our research.

Can we talk about something that's been on my mind since that Airbnb fee change announcement? Platform dependency is the biggest risk to your STR business. I know that sounds dramatic, but think about it: When Airbnb changes their fee structure, that's a hit to your bottom line that you had zero control over. When Booking . com changes their algorithm, your visibility can drop overnight. When VRBO updates their policies, you have to adapt or risk being penalized. You're building your business on rented land. And here's the scary part - it's only going to get worse. Platforms will continue to: ✖️Take bigger percentages ✖️Change algorithms unpredictably ✖️Add more restrictions ✖️Favor certain types of properties ✖️Control your guest relationships But what if you could build your own booking pipeline? What if guests found you through YOUR content, booked directly through YOUR website, and came back year after year 🤯 because of the relationship they built with YOU? This isn't about abandoning platforms entirely. They're valuable for discovery, especially when you're starting out. This is about being strategic for the long term. Building your own pipeline means: 🔥Creating content that ranks in search engines (Google, Pinterest) 🔥Developing direct relationships with past guests 🔥Building an email list of people interested in your area 🔥Establishing your brand beyond any single platform 🔥Owning your audience instead of renting it The beautiful part? The content you create for this approach works across your platforms. The transformation story that attracts direct bookings also works in your Airbnb listing. The guest journey you design for your website also improves your platform presence. The email relationship you build creates reviews that help everywhere. You're not creating more work - you're creating assets that compound. What percentage of your bookings come direct vs. through platforms? I'm curious where everyone's at with this - and whether reducing platform dependency feels urgent or overwhelming to you right now!

It is 2008. Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia are broke in San Francisco. A design conference fills every hotel. They rent out three air mattresses in their loft for $80 a night. They throw in a breakfast and just like that they have a business. They bring on Nathan Blecharczyk to build the website. They call it "AirBed & Breakfast." Standard startup story, right? Here's where it gets interesting. Investors rejected them repeatedly. VCs didn't get it. Strangers living in each other's homes seemed insane, risky, unsanitary. The market was "not large enough," they were told. Meanwhile, Airbnb's competitors were crushing it. Wimdu had massive backing from Rocket Internet, better funding, experienced teams, sophisticated engineering. On every metric (capital, talent, technology), Wimdu should have won. Yet Wimdu is now defunct. Merged into obscurity in 2016. Airbnb went public in 2020 at a $100 billion valuation. It's now the fifth-largest hotelier in the world. What Actually Happened Wimdu built what they thought the market wanted. Airbnb asked what humans actually needed. When Airbnb was hemorrhaging money (selling limited-edition cereal for $40 a box just to survive), they made one decision that mattered: they went to live in their customers' homes. They flew to New York and walked into strangers' apartments. Not to optimize. To understand. What they discovered wasn't about algorithms or interfaces. It was this: hosts were terrified. Guests were cautious. The barrier wasn't technical. It was psychological. That single insight became the architecture of everything: the host guarantee, the review system, verification. Every feature designed not to process transactions faster, but to build trust between humans with no reason to trust each other. By 2014, Airbnb had figured out something radical. They realized the problem had never been about renting houses. It was about something deeper. Airbnb launched "Belong Anywhere" as their mission, introducing a new logo called the Bélo, representing a universal symbol of belonging. The campaign shifted from simply promoting listings to creating an emotional connection with customers by promoting the authentic experiences they could have while traveling. . . . . . . Brilliance without empathy looks impressive on paper. It scales in spreadsheets. It rots in reality. The companies that win are the ones that understand humans first, then apply brilliance to solve their real problems. What's a time you've seen brilliant work fail because it lacked human understanding? I see this pattern constantly in corporate environments. The winners always understand their people before they build for them.

The Ultimate Lesson in Marketplace Innovation: The Airbnb Story When people talk about disrupting an industry, they often point to Airbnb. But the real lesson isn't just the size of the company today; it's how they started by solving an incredibly niche, urgent problem to create a new middle-class market. The Genesis: A Rent Problem & A Market Gap In 2007, founders Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia were broke and couldn't afford rent in San Francisco. A design conference was in town, and all the city’s hotels were fully booked. The opportunity wasn't to compete with hotels—it was to serve the underserved demand that hotels couldn't handle. They put three air mattresses on their floor, offered breakfast, and created a simple website: "Air Bed and Breakfast."Their first guests paid $80 each. The 'Chicken-and-Egg' Hack They quickly faced the classic "marketplace dilemma": no guests because of few listings, and no hosts because of few guests. Their survival depended on resourcefulness: 1. The $30K Pivot: To raise money when VCs rejected them, they sold limited-edition, election-themed cereal boxes ("Obama O's" and "Cap'n McCain's") for $40 each. This stunt raised enough cash to keep the lights on and proved their commitment. 2. The Trust Fix: They realized listings looked terrible (blurry phone photos). They flew to New York and personally took professional photos of their hosts' homes. This simple act of design and quality control built the trust needed for strangers to book a stay, unlocking liquidity. The Middle-Class Market Maker Airbnb succeeded because it fundamentally created a new economic engine: - For Hosts (The Supply Side): It allowed middle-class homeowners and renters to become micro-entrepreneurs, monetizing their largest underutilized asset—their home or spare room—to earn supplemental income for mortgages or tuition. - For Guests (The Demand Side): It offered a more affordable, local, and authentic travel experience, appealing directly to a new generation of travelers. The Takeaway for Builders: The most successful marketplaces don't just connect buyers and sellers; they solve a high-pain problem for a narrow, neglected niche first, build trust through obsessive focus on the user experience, and ultimately create an economic opportunity for the entire middle of the market.#MarketplaceStrategy#Entrepreneurship#Innovation#Airbnb#TwoSidedMarketplace

What's the real difference between a listing that just survives on Airbnb and an asset that thrives for years ? I think about it in two ways: some people build a "Listing," while others build an "Asset." A "Listing" competes. It relies on the algorithm, fights on price, and is always vulnerable to the new property that pops up next door. An "Asset" has its own gravity. It has a distinct brand, a system for creating incredible guest experiences, and a reputation that generates 5-star reviews on repeat. What Airbnb teaches us about website messaging When Airbnb first launched, the founders couldn’t afford rent. So they bought a couple of air mattresses and rented out their apartment to conference-goers who couldn’t find hotel rooms. No big funding. No brand. No platform. Just a really clear solution to a real problem. And it worked. Their guests got what they needed, and told others. That clarity of value is what allowed Airbnb to grow, even when investors didn’t get it. Eventually, they made their website clearer. Positioned their messaging better. Told the story in the way people needed to hear it. And today? Airbnb is valued at over £57.22 billion. But it all started with a very simple question: “Can you explain what you do in a way that makes someone say ‘I need that’?” Most professional businesses I speak to have incredible services. Real value. Great outcomes. But their websites? They sound like brochures. They’re vague. They’re trying to say everything, and end up saying nothing clearly. Here’s the lesson: 👉 Marketing isn’t about saying more. 👉 It’s about saying the right thing, to the right person, at the right time. That starts with clarity, not cleverness. Airbnb didn’t need gimmicks. They just solved a real problem, clearly. What would your website say if it was focused on solving, not selling? Speak soon, Krystal

Stas Lawrence

❌ “Meta is now the cruelest tech company out there,” wrote a Meta employee. The latest round of layoffs at#Meta has made headlines—not just because of the job losses, but because of how they were handled. Layoffs are never easy, and it’s almost inevitable that some employees will voice their frustration publicly. But it doesn’t have to be this way. Layoffs, while painful and challenging, can still be executed with empathy and transparency—reducing not only reputational risk but also long-term damage to employee morale, productivity, and company culture. Among the many layoffs in tech,#Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky set a strong example in 2020. His announcement laid out a clear, empathetic framework: 🔹 The business reasons behind the layoffs. 🔹 The guiding principles shaping decisions. 🔹 How the layoffs would be executed with fairness and dignity. 🔹 Support options for affected employees. 🔗 Read his message here: https://lnkd.in/eGFQFedh The people being let go have contributed to your company’s success. They deserve: ✅ A personal conversation, not just an impersonal email. ✅ Clarity—on why this is happening, their options, and what’s next. ✅ Redeployment opportunities, career support, and follow-up. ✅ Empathy—because how you treat them reflects your values. 🤝 A company’s culture isn’t defined by words on a wall but by actions in moments like these. Layoffs done with dignity reinforce a culture that people want to be part of—both now and in the future.#Leadership#Culture#Layoffs

Corner Office by Kai Ryssdal and Maria Hollenhorst

Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky and his golden retriever Sophie in Ann Arbor, Michigan.

Airbnb announced several updates to its platform today. The company says redesigns to search and a new feature allowing people to split up long stays are part of “the biggest change to Airbnb in a decade,” brought about in response to pandemic-era trends enabling more people to blend work and travel.
Though Airbnb was hit hard by the early months of the pandemic, the company has benefited from those shifting trends over the past two years. In a quarterly earnings announcement last week, Airbnb said total bookings have now surpassed pre-pandemic levels and long-term stays (lasting 28 days or more) have doubled since 2019.
Brian Chesky, Airbnb’s co-founder and CEO, spoke with “Marketplace” host Kai Ryssdal about the company’s decision to allow employees to “ live and work anywhere” while maintaining their current salaries and Chesky’s own announcement that he is now “living on Airbnb.” The following is an edited transcript of their conversation.
Brian Chesky: For the past two years, Airbnb has been remote. I’m 40, I live with nothing but a golden retriever. She’s got flexibility, I’ve got flexibility, and I figure, life’s short, I want to see a little more of the country. And so, for the last four months I’ve hopped around, I’ve stayed in probably a dozen Airbnbs and saw a bunch of the country. It’s been a lot of fun and it’s been a great experience.
Kai Ryssdal: Alright, so there’s a lot to unpack there. But first of all, how’s the dog doing? How does she like moving every couple of weeks?
Chesky: She seems to like it, you know, I do my best to communicate. We pick homes with yards and she likes it. It’s a lot of fun and it’s been a great experience.
Earlier this year, Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky announced he had begun “living on Airbnb,” moving to a new city every couple of weeks. Above: Chesky and his dog Sophie in Ann Arbor, Michigan
(Courtesy Airbnb/Jessica Chou)
Ryssdal: Are you booking your own stays through the app? Or do you have people who do that for you?
Chesky: I book it. And I don’t tell them who I am, although I don’t use a pseudonym either, so I’d say, half the time, they figure out it’s me, half the time they don’t. And it’s great because you kind of see all the good and, occasionally, not as good about your product, and that’s exactly how you get the feedback. I also just think that anecdotes and firsthand experiences are often more helpful than just data alone.

Listen NowSaveShare Download
Airbnb was planning an IPO back in March, but the pandemic threw a wrench in that plan.
John MacDougall/AFP via Getty Images
It has been a roller-coaster year for a lot of businesses, few more than Airbnb.The company saw an 80% drop in business last spring as the pandemic hit. It laid off a quarter of its employees, raised $2 billion in private funding and hurried the heck up and introduced Online Experiences like virtual cooking classes to try to make any money at all. But by December, it had recovered enough for a blockbuster initial public offering and a profitable third quarter.
Now the company is preparing for the return of its core business and its preexisting challenges, like being blamed for housing shortages, as well as facing new ones, like whether to house rioters planning to storm the U.S. Capitol.
Brian Chesky is the CEO of Airbnb. The following is an edited transcript of our conversation.
Brian Chesky (Photo courtesy of Airbnb)
Brian Chesky: In the wake of the Jan. 6 insurrection in Washington, D.C., a thought occurred to us, and the thought was, “Where are these people staying?” And the next thought occurred to us is, “Wait, people are going to come back to D.C.” And we said, “We’re going to cancel all reservations for the week of inauguration.” But I think we learned the lesson a long time ago that we have to take more responsibility because our product is in the real world. And that’s led to us having agreements with more than 1,000 cities around the world.
Molly Wood: I want to ask you about that, actually. Because that has been the other bubbling thing with Airbnb, this relationship with cities and whether Airbnb is partly to blame for the lack of affordable rentals. As you sort of prepare for that part of the business to come back in force, how have you rethought your relationships with cities and housing?
Chesky: I think COVID allowed everyone to take a breather. I think we got a bit of a reset on the relationship with some cities. Cities started approaching us. Some of them actually said they wanted to partner with us to get more demand because, they said, “We have major budget shortfalls now. We have major tourism shortfalls.”
Wood: So you think it’s the kind of reset where cities were like, “Oh, we need you,” as opposed to, “You, Airbnb, have to do more to deal with the question of housing supply”?

Today, I’m talking with Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky. This is Brian’s fourth time on the show, and he’s one of my favorite guests because he’s so clearly obsessed with things like company structure, design, and decision-making. You know, Decoder stuff.
We had Brian on the show last fall to talk about “founder mode,” a buzzy phrase inspired by a talk that Brian gave about his detail-oriented management style. As we were walking out of the studio, Brian told me he had some big news he was incredibly excited about but couldn’t tell me about yet. That news was a redesign of the Airbnb app with a striking new design language; new curated experiences in various cities, some led by celebrities and athletes; and a whole new services feature that lets you book things like private chefs and photographers.
You’ll hear Brian describe all this as a full-scale rethink of Airbnb, everything from how individual properties are stored in the company’s databases to how the actual company is structured, or changed, in order to get to where he wants to be five years from now.
That would be a great episode of Decoder all on its own. But if you’ve been listening to the past few episodes, you know that I’m particularly interested in what happens to services like Airbnb, Uber, and DoorDash as new kinds of AI assistants and agents get more popular. Google just announced new agent features in Chrome and in various research prototypes, Microsoft is rapidly pushing on some of the core technologies to make agentic systems happen, and there are lots and lots of demos and test projects out there showing off what the next generation of automation might be able to accomplish.
But all of those things disintermediate service providers — after all, if you can just ask an AI assistant to bring you interesting vacation listings, get you a ride to the airport, or book a private chef, you might never actually open that beautiful new Airbnb app and see all the new things they’re trying to sell you to grow their business. So Brian and I talked about this quite a bit. This will be the next set of high-stakes negotiations in tech and business, and it’s clear he’s been thinking about it a lot.
It also wouldn’t be a Brian Chesky episode if I didn’t take the time to ask him about OpenAI and its CEO, Sam Altman — Brian is close friends with Sam, and he was a...

BRIAN CHESKY: Joe and I are broke. We’re losing weight, and I didn’t have a lot of weight to lose. You know those binders that you put baseball cards in? We put credit cards in them. At this point I am $25,000 in credit card debt. Joe is tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt. So this is make or break. We need a lifeline.
REID HOFFMAN: That entrepreneur in need of a lifeline? That’s Brian Chesky, co-Founder and CEO of Airbnb, a service that lets you rent a couch for the night. Or a cabin. Or a castle. Today, Airbnb is valued at$30 billion. Eight years ago? A very different picture.
CHESKY: We have this website and maybe 50 people a day are visiting it, and we’re probably getting like 10 to 20 bookings a day. By the way, we’ve been working for a year and a half. So for anyone who’s worried their company doesn’t have enough traction, that was our traction.
HOFFMAN: It was 2008, an election year in the United States. Barack Obama was running against John McCain. Brian was at the Democratic National Convention, hatching a PR campaign for Airbnb — one that could rescue the company and their credit card bills.

So we thought, the presidential campaign is coming up. We just launched at the DNC. What if we created a Barack Obama-themed breakfast cereal? And we thought, what would a Barack Obama-themed breakfast cereal be called? Obama O’s like Cheerios, “The breakfast of change.” We thought, “Well, we want to be a nonpartisan website so we’d also need a John McCain themed cereal.” John McCain was a captain in the Navy, and so we came up with Cap’n McCain’s, like Cap’n Crunch: “A maverick in every bite.” We ended up making a thousand boxes of collectible breakfast cereal. HOFFMAN: That’s $40,000. Not bad for pocket change. And it got them through a cash crunch. But it came at a cost.

# 5 Things We Learned from Airbnb Co-founder and CEO Brian Chesky's Keynote Speech
## From Airbnb Homes to awards for members new things are coming to the global platform.
March 06 2018 4:24 PM EST
During a keynote in San Francisco, Airbnb Co-founder and CEO Brian Chesky announced that the company is significantly expanding its home business to bring accessible travel to everyone.
So, what does that mean for travelers?
As property offerings have expanded, the amount of types of properties on the platform have become increasingly diverse from treehouses to boutique hotels but it’s still only possible to navigate by three property types: Shared Space, Private Room, and Entire Home. This makes it hard for hosts to stand out and guests can’t always find what they’re looking for. To address this, Airbnb will add 4 new property types to its platform: Vacation Home, Unique Space, B&B, and Boutique. This will give greater choice of accommodation options to guests, provide greater transparency over the types of accommodation available, and help hosts showcase what’s unique about them to better match with guests’ preferences.
This feature will launch this summer. Airbnb Collections
Airbnb is launching Collections—homes for every occasion. Launching this week is Airbnb for Family and Airbnb for Work with Collections for Social stays, Weddings, Honeymoons, Group getaways, and Dinner parties coming later this year.
New Tiers - Airbnb Plus
To broaden the appeal of Airbnb to even more guests, and to recognize hosts who go above and beyond to provide outstanding hospitality, Airbnb is today launching a new tier of homes on Airbnb that have been personally verified for quality and comfort. Starting with 2,000 homes in 13 cities available to book right now, Airbnb Plus is intended for guests looking for amazing homes. Airbnb Plus homes have been inspected and verified in person against a 100+ point checklist covering cleanliness, comfort, and design. Airbnb Plus hosts benefit from top placement, in-home services such as design consultation and expert photography, and premium support. New Tiers - LuxuryFollowing last year’s acquisition of Luxury Retreats, Airbnb today previews a new standard in travel—Beyond by Airbnb. Launching this Spring, Beyond by Airbnb will offer custom-designed trips of a lifetime, including the world’s finest homes, custom experiences, and world-class hospitality. # Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky at Skift Global Forum 2024 | Expanding Airbnb Beyond Accommodations

Brian Chesky, CEO and Co-Founder of Airbnb, engages in a thought-provoking discussion with Skift CEO and Founder Rafat Ali at the 11th Skift Global Forum in New York on September 18, 2024.
Key Highlights from “Expanding Airbnb Beyond Accommodations”:
• Impact of NYC's Airbnb Ban: Chesky discusses the consequences of New York City's decision to ban short-term rentals, including a rise in hotel prices by 7.4% and an increase in average rent by 3.4%. Explore how the ban affects housing availability and affordability for locals and visitors.
• Navigating Regulations: Chesky outlines Airbnb's willingness to collaborate with city officials to find balanced solutions that benefit all stakeholders.
• Over-Tourism Solutions: The CEO defines over-tourism as too many people visiting the same place at the same time and proposes redistributing travel to lesser-known destinations.
• Strategic Expansion: Airbnb aims to go beyond short-term rentals by launching new business lines annually, signaling a shift in its operational focus.
Innovative Product Releases: The upcoming products will enhance the platform by matching hosts with property managers, creating a more streamlined rental experience.
• Growth in Experiences: Chesky highlights plans to expand the Experiences segment, emphasizing unique, local offerings to enrich travelers' journeys.
• Longer-Term Stays: The company recognizes significant growth potential in longer-term stays, catering to evolving traveler needs and preferences.
• Future Market Focus: Chesky highlights Japan as a key market for growth and discusses the strategy for catering to Chinese travelers seeking international experiences.
Join us as Chesky shares his vision for the future of travel and how Airbnb aims to create unique experiences for its users.

# Chesky on Airbnb's future in travel and beyond
Brian Chesky is far from slowing down at Airbnb.
The 43-year-old co-founder and CEO of the short term rental giant sees his company evolving to new heights.
“We're not an OTA. We do not do alternative accommodations. Alternative accommodations is what Booking.com does. We do Airbnb. It's a distinct, standalone category,” said Chesky. “Take the Airbnb model and we're going to bring it to a lot of different categories.”
He referenced Apple and Amazon’s journeys - what if Apple never launched the iPod, or Amazon never sold more than books?
“Why would Airbnb just offer homes? Why couldn't we offer significantly more things? And that's the future of this company,” Chesky said.
Chesky appeared virtually at Skift Global Forum on Wednesday and shared that Airbnb will be introducing two to three lines of “new business” each year on a go-forward basis starting next year. His intentions extend beyond travel and he reiterated that innovation is his "bread and butter."
Get a dose of digital travel in your inbox each day
“I think we're going to start with travel,” said Chesky. “Eventually we do think there's a path here to be doing more than just travel, but we're going to start with the way the consumers think about us, which is travel.”
It’s not the first time that Chesky has claimed the company will reach past the bounds of travel, he has mentioned longer term rentals, car rentals, housing and more.
But his vision is far-reaching - in terms of both creativity and potential profit, by his estimation.
“I anticipate, every year we launch two to three things that could eventually generate a billion dollars annually in revenue,” he said.
He clarified he doesn’t expect all new lines to be successful.
## “Much bigger” Experiences next May
Chesky’s appearance came on the heels of Airbnb's move last week to quietly reopen its Experiences platform to hosts, provoking a mixed response from the industry.

# White Walls

2 min read

Feb 26, 2017

--

31

Share

Our San Francisco office has very clean white walls. One day I asked why no one dared to paint them. We never told people they couldn’t. Everyone just assumed they weren’t supposed to mess with the white walls. It was at this moment that I realized that I needed to give permission to everyone to change things, whether it be the white walls, the product, or something else.

Now when I meet with new hires, here is what I say:

“Everything around you was designed by people no more experienced than you. Only four and a half years ago, we were operating out of a 3 bedroom apartment, and we had fewer resources than you have now. Back then, we dreamed of being able to hire the best people in the world. We have now hired you. You are here to change things. If we didn’t want anything to change, you would not be here.”

Never assume you can’t do something. Push yourself to redefine the boundaries. Just like Joe, Nate and I challenged the status quo, you are here to challenge us. So if you see a white wall, make your mark.

In 2015 I started an email series where each Sunday I would email our company something that was on my mind. Since then I have written nearly 100 emails in what is now called the Sunday Night Series. In the coming months I will be sharing some of these with you. This was one of the first ones.

If you visit our office today, you will notice there are no longer many white, unmarked walls.

More from series: Don’t Edit Your Imagination 7 Rejections Don’t Fuck Up the Culture

# A Week in the City of Light and Love

4 min read

Nov 16, 2015

--

28

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Our team worked into the morning calling everyone to make sure they were okay.

Yesterday I arrived back in San Francisco, along with 200 of us, from one of the longest and most emotionally draining weeks since we started Airbnb.

The week began last Sunday when I landed in Paris for the Airbnb Open, arriving at a charming 6th floor attic apartment in Saint Germain. The Airbnb Open began Thursday morning in a large arena tent that housed nearly 5,000 hosts and 645 employees. Hosts had taken the pilgrimage from nearly 110 countries, and their enthusiasm was effusive as smiles beamed from their faces.

Friday evening, I joined my family at an early employee reunion dinner at a beautiful Airbnb with our first 40 employees. It was an emotional beginning of the evening. Joe gave a toast, and we reflected on the last four and a half years together.

At 9:45pm, news started coming in of an attack in Paris. At first, we thought it was an isolated incident, so we only occasionally checked Twitter. By 10:30pm, it was clear that they were a series of coordinated attacks. Once we learned that 100 people were taken hostage in a theater, fear struck over the dinner. Our phones started buzzing with friends and loved ones wondering if we were okay. Immediately, we started thinking about our employees and hosts who were distributed throughout the city while a series of coordinated attacks were transpiring. Most remarkably, for many, the first person that contacted us was our Airbnb host.

Michael, our head of security, took over central command, and a handful of employees stayed up throughout the night so could account for all 645 of our employees. Some were just a couple doors from the attacks, and had witnessed much of the horror. We knew that one of our groups w

# How to Time Travel

Brian Chesky

2 min read

Dec 24, 2015

--

114

Listen

Share

I once met a traveler named Charlie. He came up to me and said something I will never forget. He said,

“Brian, I travel because it makes my life seem longer.”

His statement perplexed me, so I asked him to explain. He continued,

“When I am on my death bed, I want to look back at my life, and have all these vivid memories. I want them to be full and different every single day.”

I thought about this. Then I thought about my childhood. It turns out that I rode the bus to school nearly 200 days a year for more than 10 years. That’s 2,000 days. I don’t remember most of those days. They blur together.

But when I was a child, once a year, we would take a family vacation. I remember every single one of these. The first one was to St Louis. It was the first time I was on an airplane. My sister was maybe 2 or 3 years old. I remember her in her little stroller.

Each year we took a trip. Dallas. Baltimore. Chicago. Seattle. When I look back on my life and the experiences I have had, I think back to these journeys. I remember every one of them. They are vivid life experiences that I shared with the people I care about. My life is longer because of the journeys I have taken.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Me (left) with my little sister and mom on one of our vacations many years ago.

Repetition doesn’t create memories. New experiences do. Our perception of time is really driven by our perception of the unfamiliar, vivid and new. Of course, it turns out time slows down the most during life threatening experiences. [1] A safer way to slow down time is to travel. Travel is a new experience that can transport you out of your everyday routine to create memories with the ones you love.

## Introduction

In 2007, Joe and I opened our home up to the first Airbnb guests. They booked a place to stay, but they ended up with something more than just an airbed at a slightly messy apartment. They learned our favorite places to grab coffee, ate the best tacos in the city, and had friends to hang out with whenever they wanted. They were thousands of miles from where they lived, and yet they felt right at home.

What started as a way for a few friends to pay the rent has now transformed into something bigger and more meaningful than we ever imagined. And what we realized is that the Airbnb community has outgrown the original Airbnb brand. So Joe, Nate, and I did some soul-searching over the last year. We asked ourselves, “What is our mission? What is the big idea that truly defines Airbnb?”

It turns out the answer was right in front of us. For so long, people thought Airbnb was about renting houses. But really, we’re about home. You see, a house is just a space, but a home is where you belong. And what makes this global community so special is that for the very first time, you can belong anywhere.

That is the idea at the core of our company: belonging.

## Belonging

We used to take belonging for granted.

Cities used to be villages. Everyone knew each other, and everyone knew they had a place to call home. But after the mechanization and Industrial Revolution of the last century, those feelings of trust and belonging were displaced by mass-produced and impersonal travel experiences. We also stopped trusting each other. And in doing so, we lost something essential about what it means to be a community. After all, our relationships with people will always be the most meaningful part of our lives. You just need to get to know them.

That’s why Airbnb is returning us to a place where everyone can feel they belong. Like us, you may have started out thi

On Monday, October 21, 2013, I sent this letter to our entire team at Airbnb. I have decided to publish this in the event it is helpful to entrepreneurs building their cultures.

Hey team,

Our next team meeting is dedicated to Core Values, which are essential to building our culture. It occurred to me that before this meeting, I should write you a short letter on why culture is so important to Joe, Nate, and me.

After we closed our Series C with Peter Thiel in 2012, we invited him to our office. This was late last year, and we were in the Berlin room showing him various metrics. Midway through the conversation, I asked him what was the single most important piece of advice he had for us.

He replied, “Don’t fuck up the culture.”

This wasn’t what we were expecting from someone who just gave us $150M. I asked him to elaborate on this. He said one of the reasons he invested in us was our culture. But he had a somewhat cynical view that it was practically inevitable once a company gets to a certain size to “fuck it up.” Hmm.. How depressing I thought.

Were we destined to eventually “fuck up our culture?” We talked about it a bit more, and it became clear that it was possible to defend, and actually build the culture. But it had to be one of the things we were most focused on. I thought to myself, how many company CEOs are focused on culture above all else? Is it the metric they measure closest? Is it what they spend most of their hours on each week?

Culture is simply a shared way of doing something with passion.

Our culture is the foundation for our company. We may not be remembered for much after we are gone, and if Airbnb is around 100 years from now, surely we won’t be a booking website for homes. We will be far past this in our evolution (not to mention that kids 100 years from now will be asking their grandparents what websites were).

# ‘The Office As We Know It Is Over,’ Says Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky

(To receive weekly emails of conversations with the world’s top CEOs and business decisionmakers, click here.)

Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky recently announced that the company’s employees will be able to work from anywhere, including (for up to three months) overseas. He also abolished location -based pay, at least within the U.S. In the days following the announcement, Airbnb’s recruiting page received a million visitors. The company, which laid off a quarter of its staff during the pandemic, also released first quarter earnings that closely matched pre-pandemic levels.

Chesky, who has decamped from San Francisco and has been living entirely in Airbnbs this year, sat down with TIME in an Airbnb above a cosmetics store in downtown Manhattan to talk about the future of the office, corporate culture, how to redesign working spaces (he’s a graduate of the Rhode Island School of Design) and how he steered the company through its darkest days.

(For coverage of the future of work, visit TIME.com/charter and sign up for the free Charter newsletter.)

This interview has been condensed and edited for clarity.

### Is it your belief that the office is over?

I think that the office as we know it, is over. It’s kind of like an anachronistic form. It’s from a pre-digital age. If the office didn’t exist, I like to ask, would we invent it? And if we invented it, what would it be invented for? Obviously, people are going to still go to hospitals and work, people are going to still go to coffee shops and work—those spaces make complete sense. But I think that for somebody whose job is on a laptop, the question is, well, what is an office meant to do?

## Key Takeaways

- Videos
- Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky spoke at a Goldman Sachs conference on Tuesday.
- Chesky said Airbnb will be a place for workers displaced by AI to find work.
- He thinks hospitality and service jobs are “not going to be disrupted for quite a long time through AI” because people want to be served and greeted by humans.

AI could take over up to half of all entry-level, white-collar jobs within the next five years, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei predicted earlier this year. But that just means more jobs will open up in other areas, says Airbnb’s CEO, namely in service and hospitality.

At the Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference on Tuesday, Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky said that hospitality and service jobs will likely be safe from AI for the next five to 10 years — and that people will come to Airbnb to find that work.

“Services and hospitality are not going to be disrupted for quite a long time through AI,” Chesky said at the event. “I do think that a lot of what we’re doing with hosting, certainly over a 5- to 10-year period, [will] still be people-driven.”

Chesky said that the reason hospitality and service jobs will be safe from AI displacement is that people still want a human touch for some experiences.

## Introduction

In 2007, Joe and I opened our home up to the first Airbnb guests. They booked a place to stay, but they ended up with something more than just an airbed at a slightly messy apartment. They learned our favorite places to grab coffee, ate the best tacos in the city, and had friends to hang out with whenever they wanted. They were thousands of miles from where they lived, and yet they felt right at home.

What started as a way for a few friends to pay the rent has now transformed into something bigger and more meaningful than we ever imagined. And what we realized is that the Airbnb community has outgrown the original Airbnb brand. So Joe, Nate, and I did some soul-searching over the last year. We asked ourselves, “What is our mission? What is the big idea that truly defines Airbnb?”

It turns out the answer was right in front of us. For so long, people thought Airbnb was about renting houses. But really, we’re about home. You see, a house is just a space, but a home is where you belong. And what makes this global community so special is that for the very first time, you can belong anywhere.

That is the idea at the core of our company: belonging.

## Belonging

We used to take belonging for granted.

Cities used to be villages. Everyone knew each other, and everyone knew they had a place to call home. But after the mechanization and Industrial Revolution of the last century, those feelings of trust and belonging were displaced by mass-produced and impersonal travel experiences. We also stopped trusting each other. And in doing so, we lost something essential about what it means to be a community. After all, our relationships with people will always be the most meaningful part of our lives. You just need to get to know them.
