# 7 Lessons From Airbnb Co-Founder Brian Chesky’s “New Playbook”
## New-age business and career lessons beyond product management, as heard on Lenny’s podcast
Aditi Nair
8 min read
Nov 30, 2023
--
3
Listen
Share
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Photo by Riccardo Annandale on Unsplash
It’s safe to say Brian Chesky created quite the buzz a few months ago with his comments on “getting rid of classic product management”.
Curious, I followed the buzz to Chesky’s appearance on Lenny’s Podcast and ended up getting so much more out of it.
I’ve never felt the need to replay a podcast but on this occasion, I did.
Seldom, outside academics, have I heard someone speak with such rapid lucidity that it took some effort to keep up.
I was hooked.
I’ve been listening to Lenny’s podcasts for a while now so I’m not surprised that he was, once again, the curious and gracious listener.
I sensed a quiet acknowledgement of Chesky’s wide-ranging thought clarity. And it wasn’t surprising that Lenny put Chesky on the “near top” of his list of the most inspirational business leaders.
I know; We don’t need to idolise another tech leader and that’s not what this post is about.
I don’t agree with all of what Chesky has said but there’s something admirable about someone so committed to personal growth, they want to be able to change their beliefs — more on that in #7.
### 7 Takeaways
You must derive your takeaways but here are 7 of mine.
P.S.: Chesky saved his best for last.
### 1. Scaling Fast And Right.
Chesky hit the nail on the head when he talked about how “a fast-growing company can turn into a slow-moving bureaucracy”.
Faced with this predicament at AirBnb, he decided to make changes and deliberately built a functional operating model — one where teams share marketing and engineering resources where possible.
He did this so that all teams grow to “row in the same direction”, as he repeatedly stressed — an acutely under-represented ambition in my book.
To understand how this helps AirBnB, it’s important to understand “the general arc” of how scale leads to politics in the first place.
As a company expands, its new product teams will, at first, depend on shared resources. Absent these, they are forced to build their own, potentially accumulating technical debt.
New teams become divisions and division leaders are forced to advocate when faced with more dependencies.
As Chesky succinctly puts it, “your division is as successful as you are a priority”
Politics kicks in, creating distrust and restricted information flows.
In other words, bureaucracy emerges, which impedes a sense of shared accountability and results in complacency.
Many of us will find ourselves agreeing with the arc so far but most telling is his take on its culmination –
At its worst, such an environment could mean that engineering and marketing teams are so dissociated that despite successive feature releases and campaigns, customers have learned very little about the product.
Chesky believes adopting a functional model is one way to address this problem and that’s why he has redefined (not dropped) product management to incorporate marketing.
### 2. Product Management 2.0
I’ve previously written about customer-centricity and how — to quote Tony Fadell, co-creator of the iPhone — “The messaging is the product”. (See here)
At its core, that’s all Chesky is advocating for.
He is attempting to collapse the barrier between “marketing” (Product Marketing) and “engineering” (Product Managers) by mandating the latter be responsible for product marketing activities too.
“You can’t be an expert in making the product if you’re not an expert in the market of the product”, he argues.
His rationale is simple — say, you shipped a product and found it wasn’t successful, what would you change? The product itself, its messaging, the distribution channels?
By redefining product management, Chesky is forcing his PMs to build what can be marketed and sold well — a structure that could prove to be instrumental for sustaining product market fit.
After all, he argues — “If you build a great product and nobody knows about it, did you even build a product?”
The messaging is the product.
### 3. Marketing 2.0
Chesky’s analogies were thought-provoking and imaginative — reframing problem statements like only a designer could.
For instance, he likened performance marketing to a laser — sure it can light up a corner of a room but it’s not capable of brightening up all of it.
It’s not capable of building a brand.
In accounting terms too, performance marketing is typically classified as a variable cost because it’s a great way to meet targeted sales goals at manageable ROIs.
However, it doesn’t “create accumulated advantages” for the brand because it isn’t a fixed-cost marketing investment towards customer education.
Chesky advocates for companies to think about releasing features in “chapters” to build customer awareness of the broader product “story”.
Consequently, AirBnB developed a system of rolling bi-annual product releases, which all teams work towards.
Product management works with the communications team to “figure out the story” and it’s often the story that dictates the product.
Why?
Ultimately, it’s the story that will connect with customers and it’s the story that should become the North Star for internal teams.
Chesky’s views seem to be an evolution of another popular tech trend — one that promotes data and technical literacy as the chief skills for growth.
He advocates for PMs to go beyond their technical skills, understand the customer’s perception and build to market.
I’ve had “design thinking” explained to me before but I think Chesky schooled listeners on it without telling us he did.
### 4. The Significance Of Assertive Leadership
Chesky strongly believes founders should be involved “in the details”.
You might think that’s a great euphemism for micro-management, which I suspect may be true in some ways.
It also reveals a greater entrepreneurship lesson — the significance of being a leader who’s open and assertive about their vision.
Allow me to elaborate.
Chesky talks about being forced to delegate decision-making as Airbnb grew and how aforementioned politics were slowing down the company.
It’s why he decided to stop pushing decisions down.
He wanted to define a unified vision that all employees could get behind and demonstrate behaviours that became the foundations of Airbnb’s culture — a “shared consciousness” as he puts it.
Chesky believes founders shouldn’t away from asserting their vision because that’s what teams need to “row” in the same direction.
Perhaps, there’s a right time to be a democratic leader and equally to be one that brings teams together.
Chesky talks about doing so by having few management layers and going through a system of regular, transparent reviews.
“If you can’t row in the same direction, why are you all in the same company?”, he implores.
### 4. Thinking Bigger And Getting It Done.
This one gave me a completely new frame of reference.
We learn that Chesky has perpetuated a culture of “adding a zero “ at Airbnb and Lenny asks what he has learned about thinking big in the process.
For Chesky, it was not about hitting the number — it was about pushing people to visualise the problem at such a scale that it forces them to think differently about the problem.
How?
When you think about solving problems at a presently unrealised scale, you have to start by assuming the current process won’t work.
You have to deeply understand the problem and break it down into its components.
“Adding a zero — at least conceptually — helps people understand the problem”. Ask yourself what would it take to add another zero?
He advises leaders to follow through by “setting the right pace”, which isn’t governed as much by hard work as it is by decisive work.
### 6. Prioritising Happiness
When asked for tips on avoiding burnout, Chesky talked about his efforts to live in balance with health, work and relationships.
And although I fully agree with it, this isn’t about productivity hacks or dietary habits.
What I was particularly surprised by, is how deliberate Chesky was with his relationships, citing an 85-year-old study on the secret to happiness(Harvard Study of Adult Development, 1938 — 2017).
He admitted his entrepreneurship journey left him feeling isolated as he got stuck in a loop of being too busy to meet friends and them assuming he was too busy to meet.
Since then, he’s been more intentional about scheduling regular trips with his high school and college friends. He regularly visits his sister in New York and was speaking from her 2-bed flat at the time of recording.
“If you imagine your life is finite and that you’re not going to be here as long as you thought you would be — it’ll completely change the way you prioritise your time and who you spend it with”.
He relates this to business too — leaders spend too much time reacting to people, rewarding specific behaviours.
More importantly, he warns, you may end up disregarding the introverts equally deserving of your attention.
### 7. The Unexpected Secret To Staying Motivated
The clincher, for me, was Lenny’s final question – “How do you stay motivated to keep growing?”, he asks.
Chesky speaking of his child-like curiosity to learn deeply, which compels him to study the “source of things” that interest him.
He talks about being an advisor to OpenAI CEO, Sam Altman and yet admitting – the more successful he becomes, the more of a beginner he tends to feel.
Does the Co-Founder of Airbnb experience Imposter’s Syndrome? I wondered.
Turns out – he just has a very different framing of it.
When you scale certain peaks to encounter new frontiers of knowledge, you will naturally start to feel like a beginner, he explains.
To quote his timely reference to Pablo Picasso, “It took me 4 years to paint like Raphael but a lifetime to paint like a child”.
Try to view the world like one and be a beginner, he advises.
His final thoughts reveal a refreshingly earnest aspiration –
Chesky “sincerely hopes” he’s able to amend some of what he has said on the podcast because it’d mean he has learned something.
It’d mean he has something to keep him going.
How’s he going to do that? By being curious.
In closing, he quotes Bob Dylan: “An artist has to be in a constant place of becoming”, referring to his own identity as one.
### Conclusion
Chesky does not come across as the typical tech leader. I don’t condone idolising leaders but I found his earnestness relatable and his adaptability admirable, leaving much to learn and make your own. Noteworthy lessons:
- Adopt a lean, functional operating model where teams share engineering resources to scale fast and right
- Redefine product management to include marketing activities so that you build to-market
- Ensure marketing is synchronised with product releases so that customers are progressively educated
- Leaders/founders should not feel shy about asserting their vision as they establish foundational practices
- Prioritise relationships to avoid burnout and be proactive about who you spend your time with
- Don’t be afraid to be a beginner: follow your curiosity to evolve your knowledge base and stay motivated
I always learn a lot from Lenny’s Podcasts but this was probably one of the top ones on my list. I’m sure everyone had their takeaways, what were yours?
You can find this episode of “Lenny’s Podcast” on Spotify.

# The Contrarian Insights of Brian Chesky: Airbnb’s Leadership Unveiled
Anjali Pathak
7 min read
Nov 23, 2023
--
Listen
Share
This article talks about the viewpoints shared by Brian Chesky, Airbnb’s CEO, in a recent episode of Lenny Rachitsky’s Podcast.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Shot of Airbnb’s app on a mobile screen
Daniel J. Boorstin once said, ‘The greatest enemy of knowledge is not ignorance, it is the illusion of knowledge.’
Who doesn’t love contrarian thinking? Well, I definitely do!
A few days back, I began my day listening to an incredible episode of Lenny Rachitsky’s Podcast, featuring Brian Chesky, the CEO of Airbnb. The conversation delved into the perspectives around thinking new & different coupled with the need to develop new working models.
In this post, I share some pivotal aspects of the discussion which might be super helpful for almost every founder to widen their perspectives..
## A. Airbnb’s Product Management
Although Airbnb has transitioned away from a traditional Product Management function, it has not eliminated the crucial role of individuals dedicated to driving the product forward.
Instead it has,
- Combined inbound ‘Product development’ responsibilities of Product Manager with the outbound ‘Marketing’ responsibilities of Product Marketing
- Offboarded much of the Program Management functions that Product Managers may do to actual Program Managers
- Restructured into smaller & senior groups, with the most senior people acting as the Product Marketers but everyone understands how to talk about the product
In essence, Airbnb built a fully-functional model wherein senior people manage by influence and not control.
A key belief Brian shares is that one can’t build a product unless they know how to talk about the product. To be an expert in making the product, one also needs to be an expert in the its market. If a product is not working out, one should know what went wrong with it — execution, product, strategy, distribution, anything else you can think of..?
Subsequently, he addresses how divisions within a large company give rise to issues such as dependencies, politics, bureaucracy, diminished accountability, and complacency. All these factors form a vicious cycle that collectively leads to initially fast-moving companies into slower ones.
Discussing the interplay between marketing and engineering, he asserts that a key heuristic for gauging organizational well-being lies in the synergy and proximity of these two critical functions. The closer, the better!
## B. Performance Marketing
Lasers, flash bulbs, and chandeliers
Brian quotes the above metaphor used by Joe Gebbia, Airbnb’s co-founder when piqued about his thoughts on Performance Marketing.
As per his thoughts, Performance Marketing is like a laser that light ups a corner of the room, but Brand Marketing works as the chandelier you need if you want to lighten up the entire room.
Broadly, Brian sees marketing as a means of enlightening others about unique advantages. According to him, many companies predominantly emphasize brand or performance marketing. However, what often goes overlooked is Product Marketing — educating customers about the products being developed and generating awareness to ensure people know that a certain product exists!
## C. A/B Testing
Don’t do an A/B testing unless there is a hypothesis
When discussing the 2019 crisis the company navigated, which we’ll delve into shortly, he emphasizes the significance of formulating a hypothesis prior to conducting an A/B test.
One should not randomly start with A/B testing without comprehending where this whole activity stands in the entire cohesive system.
## D. Two Year Product Strategy Roadmap
Airbnb follows a dynamic two-year product plan, regularly updated every six months with releases scheduled for May and October/November; wherein entire company works in the same direction.
The model emphasises on crafting a compelling narrative as that is what dictates the product and helps in coming up with a unified, cohesive one
## E. Journey as a CEO
Brian starts off with a very strong sentiment about the role of a Chief Product Officer.
He believes that the CEO of a product/tech company ideally should act as the CPO
Then, he further describes the comprehensive approach that he adopted while acting as the CEO of Airbnb — encompassing marketing, design, operations, and a lot more excluding engineering, all while actively playing the role of a Product Manager listening to the people.
Eventually, he found himself in a scenario where listening to the voices of those he led prompted the adoption of a ‘delegating down’ model. Consequently, over the period from 2015 to 2019, the pace of progress gradually slowed.
Thinking it would be beneficial, the situation took an unexpected turn, leading to led to an ultimate slow down
## F. 2019 Crisis: Transition to a Functional Model
Progress was slow all these years and by late 2019, the conditions took a severe downturn. A pivotal factor contributing to this whole situation was the development of the ‘divisional culture’ stemming from the delegating model adopted earlier.
With no real investing in the brand; billions of spending in AdWords, huge amount of A/B testing, slow product, stagnant app with no changes, rising costs, more people being hired, individual interests popping up, irrelevant meetings, and many more unfortunate things kept on happening..
Brian found himself in a questionable situation.. and then the pandemic hit — 80% of the business lost in 8 weeks! A huge blow but an eye opener moment! That’s when he switched from reaction to action, took control, got more involved, and started with the notion of getting “in the details”
## G. In the Details…
First things first, everything needed to be documented going onwards. Yes, that was his first step to change!
Followed by more changes..
Cut down the number of projects, removed layers of management, sliding into a functional model which was more of a start-up with fewer employees and fewer senior people..
So, this is how exactly Brian drove the product at Airbnb :
- no divisional leaders, fewer employees, fewer senior people
- only start up functions — design, engineering, product, marketing & communications, sales, operations
- every executive is an expert in their functional domain
- top 30–40 employees have a continuous conversation
- metrics are a subordinate to the calendar
- a new function called Product Marketing — merged with Product M/M
- consider Program Management as a high status job
- build Marketing Communications & Creative function
- restructured the Writing function — merging UX writing with Marketing writing
- every project that ships has to be on the roadmap
- Brian reviews all the work at a cadence — week, 2 week, and so on..
- a Head Program Manager scores the projects — green/yellow/red
- make use of reviews of the work every week and identify bottlenecks
Brian underscores that maintaining a small company (as fewer as 7000 people today) and having a limited number of senior personnel proved to be highly beneficial
## H. “Leaders are into details..”
Brian says that founders have an apologetic sentiment about their preferred approach to running the company. They often seek a compromise between their vision and the preferences of those they lead. However, he asserts that this can lead to a challenging situation for everyone involved.
In reality, what matters is clarity! According to him, what people truly desire is clarity in steering the company in a specific direction, and it might not be a clever idea to appease employees.
Ever thought what is the difference between ‘micromanagement’ and ‘being into the details’?
He explains that while micromanaging could be telling people what to do, being in the details is simply being able to know what everyone’s doing. It serves as a means to ultimately figure if people are doing a good job. If you don’t know the details, how will you know if something needs improvement?
Well.. Point well taken!
## I. “Brian’s Checklist for a Functional Model”
Here is the blueprint he provides for implementing an Effective Airbnb Model :
1. CEO as CPO: Active involvement in the product
2. Interconnected Product Managers: Seamless collaboration
3. Leaders as Experts: No exclusive people managers
4. Slow Growth: Keep teams lean and efficient
5. Collaboration: Emphasize teamwork
6. Consider Launches: Encourage a proactive approach
7. Focus on Understanding: Blend data, research, and intuition
8. Engineering & Design to Founder: Product-led structure
9. Product Management: Customer understanding, storytelling
10. Product managers: Fusion of art and science
11. Interconnected Marketing & Engineering: Seamless alignment
12. Fewer Layers: Streamlined communication
13. CEO’s Direct Connection: Think of each direct-to-direct as a direct report
14. Release Narratives: Each release contributes to the company’s story
15. Unified Direction: Encourage everyone to row in the same direction
## J. “Add a Zero”
He discusses the importance of understanding the problem in order to be an ambitious company. He adds that having a first principle thinking leads to understanding the problem from different angles.
One of the ways to do this is ‘adding a zero’ — make it more bigger and better. For example, for a specific goal or problem in question; thinking of it on a 10X scale and then brainstorming into achieving those goals.
This helps to push people to come with a different mindset, approaches, thinking the other way. It ultimately helps them understand the problem in depth.
The second important factor for achieving bigger things is to ‘set the pace’. Be decisive and make fast decisions. Inculcate a culture of having a ‘bias for action’ and getting things done as fast as possible.
The role of a leader is to be able to motivate the team to see potential in them that they do not see in themselves, to push them, and to set the tempo
## K. “Avoiding burnout”
He unravels that if one decides to get into the details, it might look that it would be a hard and time-consuming shot in the initial years but that leads to setting up company’s direction continuously moving towards a certain goal. As a result, more time at one’s disposal.
Step away from the work for sometime, maintain good health — eat healthy, workout, sleep well; and maintain healthy relationships
## L. “Fun fact”
Brian wraps up by revealing a charming aspect of his past as an artist and designer prior to his tenure at Airbnb, adding a distinctive touch that sets the company apart.
He attributes the qualities he possesses, nurtured by his early passion for art — qualities such as intuition, imagination, design, and curiosity led to a mindset that embraces flexibility when constructing things around him
Instead of envisioning an organization as rigid and unidirectional, he advocates for a malleable approach. Cultivating a well-rounded perspective, rather than a biased mindset, adds a certain charm that can set a company apart. This is why, he believes there is a need for more CEOs with a background in design and graphics.
Thanks for tuning in! I hope that helps, and I anticipate your feedback! :)

# Brian Chesky on Launching Airbnb and the Challenges of Scale
Shahzaib Shafqat
6 min read
Apr 30, 2024
--
Listen
Share
Some podcasts are like treasure mines if you’re considering starting a business. One great example is the podcast in which Brian Chesky(Founder of AirBnB) talks about starting Airbnb and facing growth challenges.
He was interviewed by Reid Hoffman, the boss and brain behind LinkedIn. So, I decided to write down all the important stuff from the podcast here.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Youtube: Blitzscaling 18: Brian Chesky on Launching Airbnb and the Challenges of Scale
This way, if you’re short on time, you can quickly read through and get some useful tips without spending too much time.
### Artistic Beginnings
Brian Chesky’s journey to becoming an entrepreneur was different.
While many tech founders have engineering, computer science, or business degrees, Chesky studied art. He attended the Rhode Island School of Design (RISD), a well-known art school that helps grow creative skills.
This background in art influenced how he thinks about business and creating new things, focusing on how things look, making users happy, and designing with care.
The Leap into Entrepreneurship
Even though Chesky had art training, he never thought he’d become a business owner. He grew up in a family of social workers in upstate New York, where he didn’t even know what “entrepreneurship” meant.
The only business owner he knew was the local pizza shop owner, which didn’t interest him. This story shows how Chesky started from a simple background but achieved great things, proving success can come from surprising places.
The Influence of Design on Business Thinking
Chesky’s education at RISD instilled in him a belief that everything around him could be redesigned and improved.
This mindset was crucial when he later approached the business world, where he applied design thinking to solve complex problems and innovate within the tech industry.
His design background helped him view challenges from a unique perspective, focusing on user-centered solutions and the overall experience — principles that would later become core to Airbnb’s service.
## The Birth of Airbnb: The Idea That Sparked a Global Movement
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Airbnb’s story began in San Francisco in 2007 and was rooted in a personal problem. Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia were struggling to pay their rent. During a design conference in the city, they noticed a glaring unmet need — every hotel room was booked.
This observation led to a simple yet revolutionary idea: renting out three air mattresses in their living room and offering guests breakfast.
This concept quickly evolved into what we now know as Airbnb, a platform allowing people to list, discover, and book accommodations worldwide. The name itself, “AirBed & Breakfast,” reflects these modest beginnings.
At first, people really doubted the idea, thinking it was odd and wouldn’t last. Chesky and Gebbia faced a lot of no’s and tough times trying to start their project. To make some money for their early work, they sold funny cereal boxes named ‘Obama O’s’ and ‘Cap’n McCains’ during the 2008 U.S. election. Surprisingly, this made a lot of money and helped keep their company going.
Airbnb’s journey was not smooth; it required multiple launches. Each relaunch allowed them to refine their approach and gradually build the traction they needed.
Chesky’s mantra, “If you launch and no one notices, you can actually just keep launching,” underscores the resilience and relentless drive that defined Airbnb’s early days.
Feedback from early users was crucial for shaping Airbnb’s development. Initially, the service was intended primarily for conference attendees looking for a short-term, economical place to stay.
However, feedback highlighted the limitations of this model. Users expressed the desire for more flexibility — not just during conferences and not necessarily on air mattresses but in diverse accommodations.
This led to significant platform adjustments, broadening Airbnb’s appeal and market.
Chesky’s background in design profoundly influenced how Airbnb was built and scaled. He placed a strong emphasis on the user experience, ensuring that the interface was intuitive and the service met high aesthetic and functional standards.
This focus on design not only made the platform user-friendly but also helped in creating memorable, pleasant experiences for both hosts and guests, which was essential for the platform’s word-of-mouth growth.
Strategies for Global Expansion
As Airbnb transitioned from a startup to a global player, Chesky faced the complex task of scaling the business model while maintaining the platform’s core values and service quality. Key strategies included:
- Data Analytics: Leveraging big data to understand market dynamics and user behavior, enabling personalized experiences and efficient market matching.
- Community Building: Fostering a strong sense of community among hosts and guests, which helped in nurturing loyalty and enhancing user engagement.
- Maintaining Culture: Even as the company grew, maintaining a strong and coherent company culture was a priority. This was achieved through careful hiring practices, community events, and continuous engagement with core values.
## Airbnb’s Continuing Journey and Chesky’s Vision
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Brian Chesky
As Airbnb continues to shape the landscape of travel and accommodations, Brian Chesky’s vision for the future is rooted in the potential of the sharing economy to bring about more inclusive and accessible forms of tourism and hospitality.
Chesky envisions a world where Airbnb not only facilitates vacation rentals but also fosters a deeper understanding and connection between different cultures and communities.
He sees Airbnb expanding its role in sustainable travel, local economic empowerment, and cultural exchange, furthering its impact on how people live, work, and connect.
### Adapting to New Challenges
The future of Airbnb involves navigating the evolving regulatory landscapes and market conditions that vary by region.
Chesky emphasizes the importance of working collaboratively with local governments and communities to ensure that Airbnb’s operations contribute positively to the areas it serves.
This includes addressing concerns about housing markets and ensuring that the platform enhances, rather than disrupts, local communities.
### Technological Innovation and Expansion
Looking forward, Chesky is committed to integrating more advanced technologies to enhance the user experience and streamline operations.
This includes leveraging artificial intelligence to personalize travel experiences and using data analytics to predict market trends and user needs more accurately.
Additionally, Chesky hints at expanding Airbnb’s services to encompass other aspects of travel and living, potentially including transportation options and unique local experiences.
## Lessons Learned from Airbnb’s Journey
Chesky shares that one of the most crucial lessons from his journey is the importance of resilience. The path of entrepreneurship is fraught with challenges, from the initial skepticism around Airbnb’s concept to the myriad operational hurdles that followed.
Chesky advises budding entrepreneurs to be prepared for setbacks and to view each obstacle as an opportunity to learn and adapt.
Innovation isn’t a one-time achievement but a continuous process. Chesky stresses that Airbnb’s success was not just about a great initial idea but also about the constant improvements and innovations that followed.
Entrepreneurs should cultivate a mindset of ongoing innovation, constantly questioning the status quo and seeking better solutions.
One of Chesky’s most emphasized points is the importance of adhering to one’s core values, even as the business scales. For Airbnb, this meant always putting community and user experience at the forefront, which helped maintain trust and loyalty among users.
Chesky advises other leaders to clearly define their values early on and consistently make decisions that align with these principles.
Finally, Chesky reflects on the power of a good idea, well executed. While many people might have good ideas, the execution of these ideas is what truly differentiates successful ventures.
He encourages entrepreneurs to focus not just on the ideation phase but also on meticulous planning, execution, and refinement.

# Brian Chesky: From Renting an Air Mattress to Reimagining Global Travel
Soham Amrutkar
5 min read
Nov 29, 2025
--
Listen
Share
In October 2007, two broke designers in San Francisco stared at an empty apartment, overdue rent, and a city filled with visitors for a design conference. Hotels were sold out. Their bank accounts were nearly empty. That night, Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia inflated three air mattresses on their living room floor and created “AirBed and Breakfast.” What started as a desperate move to pay rent would evolve into Airbnb a company that transformed how the world experiences travel, belonging, and trust.
Brian Chesky grew up in Niskayuna, New York, the son of social worker parents who encouraged creativity. He wasn’t the smartest kid in class, nor the loudest, but he had an unusual eye for design. While other kids sketched superheroes, Brian sketched sneakers, furniture, and imaginary cities. That artistic sensibility later became the blueprint for one of the world’s most design centric companies.
He attended the Rhode Island School of Design (RISD), where he met Joe Gebbia a friendship that began with design debates, late night brainstorming, and the shared belief that creativity wasn’t confined to a canvas. RISD wasn’t a business school, but it taught Brian something far more valuable: the power of perspective. Design wasn’t about making things beautiful; it was about making things work better for people.
After college, Brian moved to Los Angeles but struggled to find purpose in traditional jobs. Something felt missing. The world wanted him to choose stability; he wanted to choose possibility. In 2007, he moved to San Francisco on a whim, with two suitcases and no job. That leap of faith irrational to many was the first step toward his destiny.
Life in San Francisco wasn’t glamorous. Rent kept rising. Opportunities felt scarce. But Brian and Joe shared a strange optimism. They believed constraints could inspire creativity. When a design conference filled the city and hotel rooms became scarce, Joe pitched an idea: “Why don’t we rent out our apartment? We can offer airbeds and breakfast.” Brian paused, then smiled. “Let’s design the world’s first airbed BnB.”
They bought three air mattresses and created a simple website overnight. To their surprise, they received three guests. They cooked breakfast, offered neighborhood tips, and listened to heartfelt stories from strangers who felt like family. That weekend changed their worldview. Travel wasn’t about rooms it was about belonging. Brian saw the potential: strangers could trust strangers through thoughtful design.
But good ideas don’t guarantee success. Their first launch got attention, but not momentum. Investors laughed at the concept. “Strangers sleeping on strangers’ floors?” “This isn’t a business; it’s a joke.” Rejection became their norm. Silicon Valley wasn’t ready for Brian’s vision but his resilience was stronger than skepticism.
As the 2008 recession hit, their financial struggles worsened. With maxed-out credit cards and an idea no one wanted to fund, Brian and his co-founders did something unconventional: they designed limited-edition cereal boxes called “Obama O’s” and “Cap’n McCain’s” during the U.S. election season. They sold $30,000 worth of boxes enough to keep Airbnb alive. More importantly, that hustle impressed Paul Graham of Y Combinator, who finally said: “If you can sell cereal like that, you can probably convince people to stay in strangers’ homes.”
Y Combinator gave them $20,000 small money, but huge validation. Under YC’s guidance, Brian refined Airbnb’s fundamentals. They visited hosts personally, photographed homes themselves, and built trust one user at a time. It didn’t matter how unconventional the idea was; they designed an experience that felt human, warm, and safe.
The turning point came when Airbnb shifted from air mattresses to full homes and unique stays. The platform exploded not because it was faster or cheaper but because it offered something hotels couldn’t: authenticity. Brian believed travel should feel like living, not lodging. Airbnb became the world’s largest community driven hospitality network, without owning a single property.
Challenges followed success. Cities questioned regulations. Hotel lobbies fought aggressively. Safety concerns and scandals tested trust. But Brian didn’t hide. He showed humility, apologized openly, adapted policies, and doubled down on community support. His leadership during crises revealed something rare in Silicon Valley emotional intelligence.
In 2020, the pandemic brought Airbnb to its knees. Travel halted overnight. Revenue dropped 80%. Brian faced the toughest decision of his life: laying off 25% of the company. But he did it with uncommon compassion transparent letters, extended benefits, alumni support programs. The world praised his humanity. Airbnb survived because people trusted him.
Months later, Airbnb went public in one of the biggest IPOs in history. The company that began with air mattresses now had a valuation over $100 billion. Brian Chesky became a billionaire on paper but still lived in Airbnbs, traveling the world to understand hosts and guests firsthand.
Today, Airbnb stands for something bigger than accommodation. It represents trust at scale. It represents creativity under pressure. It represents a belief that ordinary people can open extraordinary opportunities for each other. Brian proved that design isn’t just a skill it’s a superpower when applied to human connection.
His story is a reminder that breakthroughs often come from breakdowns, that constraints force innovation, and that the future belongs to creators who dare to reimagine ordinary systems. Brian Chesky didn’t invent hospitality reinvented belonging.
It all began with an overdue rent payment, three air mattresses, and an impossible idea. And it grew into a global movement that changed how millions travel, connect, and trust.
Brian Chesky didn’t just build Airbnb.He built a world where anyone can belong anywhere.
